name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْسَىٰهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ رَبِّى ۖ لَا يُجَلِّيهَا لِوَقْتِهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ ثَقُلَتْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً ۗ يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِىٌّ عَنْهَا ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (١٨٧)
```

```english
They question you concerning the Hour, when will it set in? Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with my Lord: none except Him shall manifest it at its time. It will weigh heavy on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but suddenly.’ They ask you as if you were in the know of it. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know.’ (187)
```

```arabic
قُل لَّآ أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِى نَفْعًا وَلَا ضَرًّا إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْ كُنتُ أَعْلَمُ ٱلْغَيْبَ لَٱسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَمَا مَسَّنِىَ ٱلسُّوٓءُ ۚ إِنْ أَنَا۠ إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (١٨٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself, nor [over] any harm except what Allah may wish. Had I known the Unseen, I would have acquired much good, and no ill would have befallen me. I am only a warner and bearer of good news to a people who have faith.’ (188)
```

## (Commentary)

The two verses clarify that knowledge of the Hour (الساعة) is among the matters of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — exclusively reserved for Allah, the Exalted; none knows it but He. There is no evidence whatsoever to determine its timing or to speculate on its occurrence; thus, it shall not come but suddenly. Furthermore, there is a subtle indication of its true nature through the mention of some of its descriptions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها** until His saying **إِلَّا هُوَ** — **They ask you about the Hour, when is its onset? Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with my Lord: none shall reveal its time except He. It weighs heavy on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but all of a sudden.’ They ask you as if you were in the know of it. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know.’** (7:187). The Hour (الساعة) refers to the hour of resurrection and the return to Allah for the final universal judgment. The definite article (اللام) denotes a specific reference (للعهد), though in the parlance of the Quran and the Divine Law (الشرع), it has become a technical term (كالحقيقة) for this specific meaning.

The word *mursā* (مرسى) is a noun of time and place, as well as a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) derived from "I anchored the thing" (أرسيت الشيء) when one establishes or fixes it; meaning: when is its occurrence and its establishment? *Tajliyah* (التجلية) means disclosure and manifestation (الكشف والإظهار); it is said, "He disclosed it (جلاه) and it became manifest (فانجلى)," meaning He uncovered it and it was revealed.

Thus, His saying: **لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ** means that none shall manifest it or uncover it at its appointed time and upon its occurrence except Allah, the Glorified. This indicates that its establishment, its existence, and the knowledge of it are one and the same. That is, it is preserved in the hidden depths of the Unseen with Allah, the Exalted; He uncovers it and manifests it whenever He wills, without anyone else encompassing it or it becoming manifest to any created thing. How could any thing encompass it or have it revealed to them when its actualization and manifestation necessitate the annihilation (فناء) of all things? Nothing possesses the capacity to encompass its own annihilation or to have the annihilation of its own essence (ذات) manifest to it. Moreover, the causal system (النظام السببي) governing the cosmos will be transformed upon its occurrence, and the knowledge that accompanies this current system belongs to this system itself.

From this, it becomes clear that the meaning of His saying: **ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — and Allah knows best — is the heaviness of the knowledge of it in the heavens and the earth, which is identical to the heaviness of its very existence. Therefore, there is no real fruit in the disagreement among commentators as to whether the "heaviness" of the Hour refers to the heaviness of its knowledge upon them, or the heaviness of its description upon the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth due to its hardships, punishment, reckoning, and recompense, or the heaviness of its occurrence upon them due to the folding of the heavens, the scattering of the stars, the joining of the sun and the moon, and the moving of the mountains, or that the heavens and the earth cannot endure its burden due to its magnitude and intensity.

This is because it is heavy in every respect related to it—its establishment, the knowledge of it, and its attributes—upon the heavens and the earth. They cannot endure its manifestation because it necessitates their annihilation, and a thing cannot endure its own annihilation.

From this, the reason for His saying, the Glorified: **لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً** also becomes clear. For *baghtah* (البغتة) and *faj’ah* (الفجأة) refer to the appearance of something without it being known prior to its appearance. Because of its "heaviness," no attribute of the Hour nor any part of it appears before its complete manifestation; therefore, its appearance to all things is sudden.

From here also, the meaning of the remainder of the verse becomes clear: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ** as will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها** until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: "The *hafī* (الحفي) is the one who is knowledgeable (العالم) about a thing." It seems to be derived from "I was persistent in the question" (حفيت في السؤال) when one is insistent. His saying: **كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌ** is parenthetical between "they ask you" and the prepositional phrase related to it. The original structure is: "They ask you about it as if you were knowledgeable and well-informed of it." This suggests that they repeated the question and were persistent in it; for this reason, the question and answer were repeated with a variation in wording.

In His saying the second time: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها**, there is an indication or proof that they assumed his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) first answer by the command of his Lord—**إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي**—was merely a case of referring the knowledge of what one does not know to his Lord, as is the etiquette of religion (أدب الدين). This is why he said: **عِنْدَ رَبِّي** (with my Lord) to signify servanthood (العبودية) and its duty, and they thought his saying: **لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ** was a description of its magnitude without that being linked to the impossibility of knowing its time. For all these reasons, they repeated the question so that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) might say something further about it or admit his own ignorance of it.

So Allah, the Glorified, commanded him to repeat the answer to them: **إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ** (Its knowledge is only with Allah), indicating thereby that the matter is serious and the answer is decisive. It stems from knowledge, not ignorance. The purpose is to impart the knowledge that the knowledge of it is restricted to Him, the Exalted, rather than being a mere lack of knowledge on the Prophet's part, and that referring its knowledge to his Lord was an act of fulfilling the duty of servanthood. For this reason, He changed the phrasing from the first answer **عِنْدَ رَبِّي** (with my Lord) to **عِنْدَ اللهِ** (with Allah) in this second answer.

Then He said: **وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** (but most people do not know), referring to their ignorance of the meaning of His saying: **إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي**. For they, due to their familiarity with the senses and the sensible world, measure everything they hear against the sensible; they generalize its judgment upon it, imagining that everything described to them in some way is within their capacity to know and encompass. They think that if there is a matter hidden from them, it is only hidden through concealment (الكتمان), and that if it were revealed to them, they would encompass it with knowledge like all other sensible matters they possess. Their analogy was mistaken and the matter became confused for them, for some things in the Unseen—among them the Hour—cannot be endured by the knowledge of anyone except Allah, the Glorified.

It has become clear from the verse that knowledge of the Hour is something that nothing can endure except Allah, the Glorified; likewise, the reality of its attributes and descriptions, for all of them are heavy with its heaviness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي نَفْعاً وَلا ضَرًّا إِلَّا ما شاءَ اللهُ** until the end of the verse. **Say, ‘I have no control over any benefit for myself, nor any harm, except what Allah may wish. Had I known the Unseen, I would have acquired much good, and no ill would have touched me. I am nothing but a warner and a bearer of good news for a people who have faith.’** (7:188). Since their questioning him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the Unseen carried the implication that his claim to prophethood was a claim to knowledge of the Unseen—and none truly knows the Unseen except Him, the Exalted, except through divine revelation and teaching—He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to disassociate himself from any claim to independent knowledge of the Unseen.

The fundamental reason for the exclusivity of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted, is that anything other than Him, whatever it may be, is limited in existence (محدود الوجود). It has no access to what is outside of it and hidden from it, insofar as it is hidden. Nothing is unlimited and infinite, encompassing all things, except Allah, the Glorified; thus, to Him belongs the knowledge of the Unseen.

However, since those questioners were unable to understand this reason given their simple and common understandings, He commanded him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to speak to them in a way they could grasp. This is that knowledge of the Unseen guides a person to every good and away from every evil. Customary experience rejects that someone could know good and evil and be guided to their occurrences, yet not utilize that for his own benefit. If a person does not acquire much good and is not protected from evil, how can he know the Unseen?

Thus, His saying at the beginning of the verse: **قُلْ لا أَمْلِكُ لِنَفْسِي** is a description of himself that contradicts the result of knowing the Unseen. Then His saying: **وَلَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ** is an explanation of the result of knowing the Unseen, so that from these two premises, the conclusion is his lack of independent knowledge of the Unseen. Finally, His saying: **إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ** is a clarification of the reality of his state regarding the messengership he claims, without there being any other claim alongside it.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها**, he said: "The Quraysh sent al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi, al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn Kaladah, and Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt to Najran to learn questions from the Jewish scholars to ask the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Among what they asked Muhammad was: 'When will the Hour occur?' So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: **يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها**."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Khalaf ibn Hammad, from a man, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Allah says in His Book: **وَلَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ وَما مَسَّنِيَ السُّوءُ**—meaning poverty (الفقر)."

**I say:** It was also narrated by al-Saduq in *al-Ma'ani* with his chain to Khalaf ibn Hammad from a man from him (peace be upon him). It was also narrated by Husayn ibn Bistam in *Tibb al-A'immah* from Jabir ibn Yazid from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).
